channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: As Trump Heads to Beijing, China Is ‘Locked and Loaded’ for a Fight
item.description: Beijing is signaling that it is ready for a trade showdown, and it is building up a legal arsenal in preparation.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:description: The port in Ningbo, China. “We are on the brink of a much more frequent or widespread use of Chinese countermeasures against U.S. sanctions,” said Andrew Gillholm, a China expert at Control Risks.

item.title: What Middle Powers Fear from the Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: Asian nations worry that the president might trade security commitments for better economic terms with China during his planned meeting with Xi Jinping this week.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: President Trump and Xi Jinping after their meeting in Busan, South Korea, in 2025.

item.title: Lawmakers Urge Trump to Move Ahead on Delayed Arms Sale to Taiwan
item.description: The Trump administration has held up the sale for months ahead of President Trump’s meeting this week with President Xi Jinping of China.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: From left, Senators Jeanne Shaheen, John Curtis, Thom Tillis and Jacky Rosen in Taiwan in March. All four signed a letter to President Trump urging him to move forward with the Taiwan arms sale.

item.title: Trump and Xi Are Set to Meet Again. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The war in Iran, trade, artificial intelligence and Taiwan are expected to be on the agenda. But expectations are modest.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, in Busan, South Korea, in 2025.

item.title: Trump Rejects Iran’s Offer, and 17 Passengers Exposed to Hantavirus Return to U.S.
item.description: Plus, how pharma swag became “one of the white whales of thrifting.”
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Alexandra Stevenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran this month. The United States and Iran have been discussing an extension to the cease-fire and a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Oil Prices Rise as Prospects for U.S.-Iran Peace Deal Fizzle
item.description: Oil prices rose and stock futures ticked down as investors reacted after the two sides failed to agree on a U.S.-Iran peace deal.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: American Passengers Exposed to Hantavirus Land in U.S.
item.description: Seventeen Americans from a cruise ship that faced a hantavirus outbreak arrived in Nebraska and will be monitored at a quarantine center. One tested “mildly” positive for the Andes virus, health officials said.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao and Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Passengers being evacuated from the MV Hondius on Sunday.

item.title: Where Are the Passengers of the Hantavirus-Hit Cruise Ship Now?
item.description: Nearly two dozen countries were moving to repatriate their citizens who were aboard the cruise ship where three passengers died in a hantavirus outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: British citizens repatriated from the MV Hondius arriving at a hospital in northwest England on Sunday.

item.title: What to Know About the Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: The health authorities are working to calm fears and contain the rare virus. Three passengers died after traveling on the ship, and several others fell ill or tested positive.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg, Claire Moses and Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.

item.title: How Israel Turned Eurovision’s Stage Into a Soft Power Tool
item.description: Israel’s efforts to influence Eurovision’s vote were broader and started years earlier than previously known.
item.dc:creator: Mara Hvistendahl and Alex Marshall
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: How to Win Eurovision With Just a Few Hundred Voters
item.description: Exclusive voting data shows that, despite Eurovision’s assurances, an Israeli campaign could easily have influenced last year’s contest.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Mara Hvistendahl
item.media:description: Yuval Raphael of Israel topped last year’s public vote and almost won Eurovision. Contest organizers don’t release detailed vote data.

item.title: The Top Restaurant Trends in New York City for 2026
item.description: Hip crustaceans, dessert trends and the never-ending reservation battle were among the things our chief critic took note of.
item.dc:creator: Ligaya Mishan

item.title: The Best New York City Neighborhoods for Restaurants
item.description: Great meals can be found in virtually any corner of the five boroughs, but here are some areas that stood out to our critic.
item.dc:creator: Ligaya Mishan
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times

item.title: A List of 100 Best Restaurants? For These Marathon Eaters, It’s a Dare.
item.description: As rankings proliferate, a cadre of completists have made it their mission to sample every single place on them.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells
item.media:credit: Marissa Alper for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Hunter with the chef Jean-Georges Vongerichten at his Jean-Georges restaurant in New York, which has three Michelin stars. Ms. Hunter tries to eat there once a month.

item.title: They Were Promised New Septic Tanks. Trump Called It ‘Illegal DEI.’
item.description: The Justice Department ended a deal that had helped fund a solution to the sewage crisis in rural Alabama. “Almost like we are starting all over again,” one activist said.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pipe carrying sewage away from a home in Hayneville, Ala.

item.title: As Coal Rebounds, More Toxic Mercury Is in the Air
item.description: Coal-burning power plants released more mercury last year, according to an analysis by The Times. It reverses a downward trend of emissions of a metal that interferes with brain development.
item.dc:creator: Irena Hwang and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: Plant Bowen, a coal-fired power plant near Cartersville, Ga., is one of dozens of coal operations to receive presidential exemptions from meeting stricter emissions rules.

item.title: Scientists Press Congress on Dismissal of National Science Foundation Board and Research Funding
item.description: The slowed pace of grants by the National Science Foundation, under attack from the White House, could put the United States at a disadvantage with China, the scientists warned.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:description: The headquarters of the National Science Foundation in May last year, before the Trump administration announced it was moving the agency out of the building.

item.title: Accusations of Shenanigans Fly in Nebraska’s Senate Race
item.description: One Democrat is accused of being a Republican in disguise. Another plans to drop out to boost an independent. It’s primary eve in one of the country’s most unusual midterm contests.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Walker Pickering for The New York Times
item.media:description: The erosion of the Democratic Party brand in the Midwest and Great Plains has led to independent candidates’ sometimes mounting more credible campaigns in rural states like Nebraska.

item.title: How Nebraska’s ‘Blue Dot’ Roiled a Democratic House Primary
item.description: A speck of blue on an otherwise red area in some presidential elections, the dot is now dividing candidates in a key midterm contest.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Nebraska isn’t a swing state, but it can still draw attention in the national conversation in presidential years.

item.title: Ukraine Looks to Step Away From Trump and U.S.
item.description: With peace talks on ice and Ukraine now more self-reliant, President Volodymyr Zelensky seems to be stepping away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker, Constant Méheut and Maria Varenikova
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine in Yerevan, Armenia, last week. Mr. Zelensky has publicly criticized the United States in ways that would have been unthinkable last year.

item.title: Putin’s Forces Are Barely Inching Along on the Battlefield
item.description: The Russian military has yet to solve a fundamental problem: how to make big advances in eastern Ukraine when drones are everywhere.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne, Cassandra Vinograd and Milana Mazaeva
item.media:description: An Ukrainian soldier in the besieged eastern city of Kostiantynivka early this year.

item.title: A Single Infusion Could Suppress H.I.V. for Years, Study Suggests
item.description: A study of a few patients, to be presented this week, showed promise for a type of therapy that has already cured some blood cancers.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:description: A study led by an H.I.V. expert at the University of California, San Francisco, could offer a path toward a lasting H.I.V. treatment.

item.title: As Visa Policies Tighten, International Students Find Tougher Job Market
item.description: Students say they have been passed over for jobs and interviews because of visa restrictions. Some have a Plan B: leaving the United States.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin and Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s just getting unfriendlier and unfriendlier,” said Caroline Liu, a Chinese citizen and student at M.I.T.

item.title: Airbnb Turns to Black Leaders in Its Bid to Make a Comeback in New York
item.description: The multibillion-dollar corporation is partnering with influential Black pastors as it seeks to regain a foothold in the city’s short-term rental market.
item.dc:creator: Maya King and Sally Goldenberg
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Rev. Al Sharpton favors loosening restrictions on Airbnb rentals in New York.

item.title: Need to Hire an Architect or Designer? Try Dumbo.
item.description: A Brooklyn neighborhood with waterfront views and pricey apartments has become New York City’s latest design hub, with more than 160 architecture and design firms.
item.dc:creator: Winnie Hu
item.media:credit: Brittainy Newman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dumbo is one of the most expensive residential neighborhoods in New York City, but office space rents at a discount compared with Manhattan.

item.title: Cursive Club, Where Students Learn With a Flourish
item.description: Students are practicing cursive in clubs after school and in libraries after it was cut from the Common Core curriculum. Some states are reintroducing it into schools.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk

item.title: The Silence That Meets the Rape of Palestinians
item.description: Male and female Palestinians describe brutal sexual abuse at the hands of Israel’s prison guards, soldiers, settlers and interrogators.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Samar Hazboun for The New York Times
item.media:description: Suhaib Abualkebash.

item.title: Bill Cassidy Sold His Soul and Got Nothing in Return
item.description: A doctor and senator who voted to confirm Robert Kennedy still was snubbed by Trump in the Republican primary in Louisiana.
item.dc:creator: Gary Sernovitz

item.title: It’s All Connected
item.description: We look into the body’s circulatory systems.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Tell us Your Games Streak Story
item.description: We need your help for an upcoming article.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: The Fight to Euthanize Pablo Escobar’s Hippos in Colombia
item.description: Colombia is planning to cull a population of wild hippos, the offspring of the drug lord’s pets, dividing a town where hippos are the main draw.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Esteban Vanegas
item.media:credit: Esteban Vanegas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A male hippopotamus walking in Doradal, Colombia. Despite their bulk, they can easily outrun humans.

item.title: Retail Giants Spend Billions to Make Stores Look Nicer
item.description: Walmart, Target and Dollar General are among those investing in remodeling thousands of existing stores, even as shoppers take their dollars online.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: A renovated Target store in Paramus, N.J. The improvements are part of a $5 billion capital investment plan at the chain.

item.title: Abraham Foxman, Warrior Against Antisemitism, Dies at 86
item.description: For almost three decades, he led the Anti-Defamation League, winning access to presidents and prime ministers and meeting with Pope John Paul II.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger

item.title: Thailand’s Thaksin Shinawatra Is Out of Prison. What Will He Do Now?
item.description: Mr. Thaksin has long been a foil to Thailand’s conservative establishment. He still holds significant influence, with his political party a member of the governing coalition.
item.dc:creator: Sui-Lee Wee and Kittiphum Sringammuang
item.media:description: Thaksin Shinawatra, a former prime minister of Thailand, leaving prison in Bangkok on Monday. He was paroled after serving eight months of a one-year sentence.
